patterns.name: ECU Firmware Extraction via Debug Port
patterns.attack_vector: Physical access to JTAG or SWD debug interface on target ECU to dump firmware for reverse engineering
patterns.impact: Firmware binary exposed, enabling discovery of cryptographic keys, proprietary algorithms, and vulnerabilities for further attacks
known_mitigations: Disable JTAG/SWD in production fuses
Use secure boot with encrypted firmware storage
Implement HSM-backed key storage separate from application processor
patterns.description: An attacker with physical access to an ECU extracts firmware through exposed debug interfaces such as JTAG or SWD. The attacker connects a debug probe to test points on the PCB, halts the processor, and reads flash memory contents. The extracted binary is then analyzed using disassemblers and decompilers to identify cryptographic keys, authentication bypass paths, and protocol vulnerabilities. This attack is foundational — the information gained enables more sophisticated remote attacks against the vehicle fleet.
prerequisites: Debug probe compatible with target architecture
detection_methods: Debug port access logging via HSM
Runtime integrity verification detecting halted processor

patterns.name: Voltage Glitch Fault Injection on ECU
patterns.attack_vector: Controlled voltage glitching on power rail or clock line to bypass secure boot verification
patterns.impact: Secure boot bypassed, allowing execution of unsigned or modified firmware on the ECU
known_mitigations: Implement voltage and clock glitch detectors in silicon
Use redundant signature verification with temporal separation
Design boot ROM with glitch-resistant instruction sequences
patterns.description: An attacker uses precise voltage manipulation on the ECU power rail to cause instruction skips during secure boot signature verification. By timing a brief voltage drop to coincide with the branch instruction after signature check, the attacker forces the processor to skip the failure path and proceed with boot. This requires detailed knowledge of the boot sequence timing, typically obtained through prior firmware extraction. Successful attack allows running arbitrary firmware.

patterns.name: Side-Channel Key Extraction from ECU
patterns.attack_vector: Power analysis or electromagnetic emanation monitoring during cryptographic operations to extract secret keys
patterns.impact: Cryptographic keys extracted, enabling message forgery, firmware signing bypass, or authentication impersonation
known_mitigations: Use HSM with side-channel resistant implementations
Apply masking and shuffling countermeasures in software crypto
Implement constant-time cryptographic algorithms
patterns.description: An attacker monitors power consumption or electromagnetic emissions from an ECU during cryptographic operations. Using differential power analysis (DPA) or correlation power analysis (CPA), statistical methods reveal secret key bits by correlating power traces with hypothetical intermediate values. Thousands of traces are collected during repeated operations, then analyzed offline. This attack targets ECUs that perform cryptography in software without side-channel countermeasures.
prerequisites: Ability to trigger repeated cryptographic operations
Power analysis equipment and statistical analysis software
detection_methods: Monitoring for unusual repeated operation patterns
Anomaly detection on cryptographic operation frequency

patterns.name: Supply Chain Firmware Implant in ECU
patterns.attack_vector: Compromise of ECU supplier build pipeline to inject backdoor code into production firmware before delivery to OEM
patterns.impact: Backdoored ECUs deployed across vehicle fleet, enabling persistent remote access or data exfiltration from production vehicles
known_mitigations: Implement SBOM validation and binary reproducibility checks
Require code signing from OEM-controlled HSM after independent build verification
Conduct regular supply chain security audits per ISO 21434 clause 7
patterns.description: An attacker compromises a tier-1 or tier-2 supplier's CI/CD pipeline and injects malicious code into ECU firmware during the build process. The implant is designed to be dormant during standard testing and activates only under specific runtime conditions. Since the OEM trusts the supplier's build output, the backdoored firmware passes acceptance testing and is installed in production vehicles. Detection requires binary analysis comparing supplier output against independently built reference images.
prerequisites: Knowledge of firmware architecture and test procedures
Ability to maintain persistent access to build systems
detection_methods: Runtime behavior monitoring for anomalous network connections

patterns.name: ECU Bootloader Version Downgrade
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting weak anti-rollback mechanisms to flash older, vulnerable bootloader version onto ECU
patterns.impact: Known vulnerabilities in older bootloader version become exploitable, undermining all higher-layer security measures
known_mitigations: Implement monotonic hardware anti-rollback counters in eFuse or RPMB
Verify bootloader version against minimum version stored in HSM
Log all flash operations to tamper-evident storage
patterns.description: An attacker exploits an ECU that uses software-only version tracking to install an older bootloader with known vulnerabilities. The attacker accesses the diagnostic interface, enters a programming session, and flashes a previously captured bootloader image. Because the version counter is stored in writable flash rather than one-time-programmable memory, the counter can be reset along with the bootloader. The older bootloader may lack signature verification, have authentication bypasses, or contain buffer overflows.

patterns.name: ECU Runtime Memory Corruption
patterns.attack_vector: Buffer overflow or format string vulnerability in ECU application code triggered via crafted CAN/UDS messages
patterns.impact: Arbitrary code execution on ECU, enabling manipulation of safety-critical functions or persistent backdoor installation
known_mitigations: Use memory-safe languages or strict coding standards (MISRA C)
Enable MPU/MMU stack protection and NX regions
Implement input validation and length checks on all bus message handlers
patterns.description: An attacker sends specially crafted CAN messages or UDS diagnostic requests that overflow fixed-size buffers in the ECU application layer. By controlling the overflow data, the attacker overwrites return addresses or function pointers to redirect execution. On ECUs without memory protection (common in low-cost microcontrollers), this enables full code execution. The crafted messages are developed through fuzzing the target ECU on a bench setup after firmware extraction.
detection_methods: CAN IDS detecting anomalous message patterns
Watchdog timer detecting execution flow anomalies

patterns.name: ECU Calibration Data Tampering
patterns.attack_vector: Modifying calibration parameters in engine or ADAS ECU via diagnostic services to alter vehicle behavior
patterns.impact: Vehicle performance altered — emissions compliance violated, safety margins reduced, or engine damage caused by incorrect parameters
known_mitigations: Authenticate calibration write operations with backend authorization
Store calibration checksums in HSM
Implement calibration integrity monitoring with periodic self-checks
patterns.description: An attacker uses standard diagnostic protocols (UDS WriteDataByIdentifier 0x2E or RequestDownload 0x34) to modify calibration parameters stored in ECU non-volatile memory. Targets include fuel injection timing, emission control thresholds, ADAS detection confidence levels, and braking force distribution tables. Many ECUs protect calibration data only with security access (seed-key) authentication, which is often defeated through key extraction or algorithmic weakness.
prerequisites: Diagnostic access to vehicle OBD-II port
Valid or bypassed security access credentials

patterns.name: ECU Secure Boot TOCTOU Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Time-of-check-to-time-of-use race condition during secure boot to swap verified image with malicious payload
patterns.impact: Modified firmware executes despite secure boot verification passing, undermining entire chain of trust
known_mitigations: Execute boot image directly from verified memory region without copy
Use hardware-enforced memory locking after verification
Implement boot chain with hardware root of trust (e.g., ARM TrustZone secure boot)
patterns.description: An attacker exploits a timing gap between when the bootloader verifies a firmware image signature and when it transfers execution to that image. During this window, the attacker uses DMA-capable hardware or a compromised co-processor to modify the verified image in RAM. The secure boot check passes because the original image was valid, but the executed code is the attacker's payload. This requires precise timing and detailed knowledge of the boot sequence memory layout.

patterns.name: ECU Debug Authentication Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Bypassing or resetting debug authentication on ECU microcontroller through undocumented commands or silicon bugs
patterns.impact: Full debug access to production ECU, enabling memory read/write, breakpoints, and firmware extraction despite production lockdown
known_mitigations: Use silicon with no known debug bypass vulnerabilities
Permanently disable debug via hardware fuses during manufacturing
Implement secondary authentication via HSM for any remaining debug paths
patterns.description: An attacker exploits known or newly discovered silicon-level vulnerabilities to bypass debug authentication on production-locked microcontrollers. Common techniques include voltage glitching during the debug authentication check, exploiting undocumented test modes left active by the silicon vendor, or using ROM bootloader vulnerabilities to re-enable debug access. Once debug is unlocked, the attacker gains full control over the processor, reading all memory including HSM communication buffers.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target microcontroller silicon revision
Physical access to ECU debug port
detection_methods: Manufacturing line verification of fuse state

patterns.name: ECU CAN Protocol Stack Fuzzing Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Sending malformed CAN frames to trigger crashes or unexpected behavior in ECU communication stack
patterns.impact: ECU crash, reset, or degraded mode entry — potentially causing loss of vehicle function or safety-critical failure
known_mitigations: Implement robust input validation on all CAN message handlers
Use AUTOSAR defensive programming patterns
Test with automotive protocol fuzzers during development
patterns.description: An attacker connects to the CAN bus and sends thousands of malformed frames with invalid DLC values, unexpected IDs, and corrupted payloads to every reachable ECU. The goal is to trigger unhandled exceptions, buffer overflows, or assertion failures in the CAN protocol stack. ECUs with poorly implemented AUTOSAR COM stacks or custom parsing code may crash and enter a safe-state or reset loop, causing denial of service for critical vehicle functions. Fuzzing tools like CANalyzer scripts or custom hardware automate this process.
prerequisites: Physical access to CAN bus via OBD-II or splice
detection_methods: CAN IDS detecting abnormal frame rates and patterns

patterns.name: Gateway CAN Firewall Rule Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Crafting CAN messages that exploit incomplete firewall rules to pass unauthorized frames between network segments
patterns.impact: Attacker on low-security bus segment reaches safety-critical ECUs on isolated high-security segment
known_mitigations: Implement default-deny firewall policy on gateway
Validate message content not just CAN IDs
Regular review and testing of gateway filter rules against vehicle DBC files
patterns.description: A central gateway ECU routes CAN messages between vehicle network segments (powertrain, chassis, infotainment, diagnostics). The firewall rules typically filter by CAN ID, allowing only expected messages to cross segment boundaries. An attacker exploits gaps in these rules — IDs that are valid on both segments, newly added ECUs whose IDs were not included in rules, or CAN-FD frames that bypass classic CAN filters. Successful bypass allows injection of spoofed messages onto safety-critical bus segments from the less-protected infotainment network.
prerequisites: Access to any CAN bus segment
Knowledge of gateway routing table or DBC files
detection_methods: Gateway logging of filtered and forwarded frames
Anomaly detection on cross-segment traffic volume
Periodic rule coverage analysis against DBC

patterns.name: Gateway Resource Exhaustion Denial of Service
patterns.attack_vector: Flooding the gateway with high volumes of legitimate-looking CAN traffic to exhaust processing resources
patterns.impact: Gateway unable to forward legitimate messages, causing communication blackout between vehicle network segments
known_mitigations: Implement rate limiting per source and per CAN ID
Use hardware-accelerated message filtering
Design gateway with sufficient processing headroom for worst-case bus load
patterns.description: An attacker floods one or more CAN bus segments with high-priority messages, forcing the gateway to process and filter each frame. Since CAN arbitration is priority-based, high-priority flood frames win bus access over legitimate lower-priority messages. The gateway's CPU becomes saturated attempting to filter the flood, delaying or dropping forwarding of legitimate cross-segment messages. This can cause timeout errors in ECU-to-ECU communication, triggering diagnostic trouble codes and potentially forcing safety-critical systems into degraded mode.
prerequisites: Access to any CAN bus segment
CAN interface capable of sustained high-rate transmission
detection_methods: Bus load monitoring exceeding threshold alerts

patterns.name: Gateway Routing Table Manipulation
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting diagnostic services to modify gateway message routing configuration, redirecting or blocking traffic flows
patterns.impact: Vehicle CAN routing altered — messages misrouted, dropped, or redirected to attacker-controlled ECU for interception
known_mitigations: Store routing table in write-protected flash with HSM-verified integrity
Require backend authorization for any routing configuration changes
Implement routing table integrity check on every gateway boot
patterns.description: An attacker uses UDS WriteDataByIdentifier or routine control services on the gateway ECU to modify its internal routing table. This changes which CAN messages are forwarded between segments, potentially blocking safety-critical traffic or redirecting sensor data to a compromised ECU for modification before forwarding. Many gateways store routing configurations in NVM that can be updated through standard diagnostic services with only seed-key authentication protecting the write operation.
detection_methods: Routing table hash verification on boot
ECU communication pattern monitoring for route changes

patterns.name: Gateway Ethernet VLAN Hopping
patterns.attack_vector: Double-tagged 802.1Q frames or switch spoofing to bypass VLAN segmentation on automotive Ethernet gateway
patterns.impact: Attacker jumps from infotainment VLAN to safety-critical ADAS VLAN, bypassing network segmentation
known_mitigations: Disable trunk ports on all ECU-facing interfaces
Implement 802.1X port-based authentication
Use MACsec for link-layer encryption and authentication on inter-switch links
patterns.description: Automotive Ethernet networks increasingly use VLAN segmentation to isolate domains (infotainment, ADAS, powertrain). An attacker connected to the infotainment VLAN crafts double-tagged 802.1Q frames. The gateway switch strips the outer tag and forwards the inner-tagged frame to the target VLAN. This classic IT network attack applies directly to automotive Ethernet when switches are misconfigured with native VLAN trunking enabled on access ports. Once on the target VLAN, the attacker can inject or sniff ADAS sensor data.
prerequisites: Access to an automotive Ethernet port
Ability to craft custom Ethernet frames

patterns.name: Gateway SOME/IP Service Discovery Hijack
patterns.attack_vector: Sending fraudulent SOME/IP-SD offer messages to redirect service consumers to attacker-controlled endpoint
patterns.impact: Service consumers connect to attacker instead of legitimate service provider, enabling data interception or response manipulation
known_mitigations: Implement SOME/IP service authentication
Use static service endpoint configuration for safety-critical services
Deploy SOME/IP firewall rules on gateway filtering SD messages
patterns.description: SOME/IP Service Discovery (SD) uses multicast to advertise available services on automotive Ethernet. An attacker on the network sends forged SD Offer messages claiming to provide a service (e.g., camera image processing) with higher priority or faster response time than the legitimate provider. Consumer ECUs connect to the attacker's endpoint instead. The attacker can then intercept requests, modify responses, or inject malicious data. This is a direct analogue of ARP spoofing adapted to the automotive service-oriented architecture.
prerequisites: Knowledge of SOME/IP service IDs and configurations

patterns.name: Gateway Firmware Rollback Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Downgrading gateway firmware to older version with known vulnerabilities through diagnostic reprogramming
patterns.impact: Gateway security features reverted — firewall rules, SecOC support, or IDS capabilities removed, exposing entire vehicle network
known_mitigations: Hardware anti-rollback counter in eFuse/RPMB
Backend authorization required for gateway reprogramming
Post-flash verification reporting to OEM cloud
patterns.description: The gateway ECU is the critical security enforcement point in the vehicle network. An attacker uses diagnostic services to flash an older gateway firmware version that lacks recently added security features such as CAN IDS, SecOC enforcement, or updated firewall rules. If the gateway uses software-based version tracking rather than hardware monotonic counters, the downgrade succeeds. The older firmware processes all traffic without modern security checks, opening the entire vehicle network to attacks that were previously mitigated.
prerequisites: Captured copy of older gateway firmware
Bypassed security access or stolen credentials
detection_methods: Backend firmware version tracking discrepancy alerts
Remote attestation of gateway security feature set

patterns.name: Gateway CAN-to-Ethernet Translation Exploit
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting protocol translation logic in gateway to inject malformed data during CAN-to-Ethernet or Ethernet-to-CAN conversion
patterns.impact: Data corruption during protocol translation causing ECU misinterpretation of sensor values or control commands
known_mitigations: Validate data semantics during translation, not just format
Implement end-to-end integrity checks (E2E protection) across translation boundary
Fuzz-test all translation paths during development
patterns.description: Modern vehicles use gateways that translate messages between CAN and automotive Ethernet domains. The translation logic must parse CAN signals, repack them into SOME/IP payloads, and vice versa. An attacker crafts CAN messages with valid IDs but unusual signal combinations that exploit parsing bugs in the translation layer. For example, a CAN message with a signal value at the maximum range boundary might overflow when converted to a differently-sized Ethernet payload field. The corrupted translated message reaches the destination ECU with incorrect values.
prerequisites: Knowledge of gateway signal translation mappings
Access to CAN bus segment feeding the gateway
Ability to analyze Ethernet output for verification
detection_methods: E2E protection checksum failures at receiving ECU
Signal range validation at translation boundary

patterns.name: Gateway SecOC Key Manager Compromise
patterns.attack_vector: Attacking the key management function hosted on gateway ECU to extract or manipulate SecOC keys for all ECUs
patterns.impact: All SecOC keys compromised, enabling message forgery and authentication bypass across entire vehicle network
known_mitigations: Isolate key manager in dedicated HSM partition
Implement key hierarchy so compromise of distribution keys does not expose master keys
Use per-ECU derived keys to limit blast radius
patterns.description: The gateway ECU often hosts the SecOC Key Manager function responsible for distributing symmetric keys to all ECUs on the bus. An attacker who compromises the key manager through firmware exploitation or physical attack on the gateway HSM can extract the key distribution material. With these keys, the attacker can forge authenticated CAN messages for any ECU, bypass SecOC verification, and inject arbitrary commands on any bus segment. The centralized key management design makes the gateway a high-value single point of failure for network authentication.
prerequisites: Firmware exploitation capability or HSM attack tools
detection_methods: Periodic key usage audit against expected patterns

patterns.name: Gateway IDS Evasion via Slow-Rate Injection
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting malicious CAN frames at rates below IDS detection thresholds to avoid triggering anomaly alerts
patterns.impact: Malicious messages delivered to target ECUs without detection, bypassing gateway intrusion detection system
known_mitigations: Implement content-based IDS in addition to rate-based detection
Use SecOC authentication to detect unauthorized messages regardless of rate
Correlate IDS alerts across multiple detection points
patterns.description: Gateway-based CAN intrusion detection systems typically monitor for anomalous message rates, new CAN IDs, or statistical deviations in bus traffic patterns. An attacker evades these checks by injecting malicious frames at rates that match expected traffic patterns — for example, inserting one spoofed steering angle message per normal transmission cycle. By staying within statistical norms, the injected frames blend with legitimate traffic. This requires prior observation of normal bus traffic patterns to calibrate injection timing and rate.
prerequisites: Prior CAN bus traffic capture and analysis
Precise timing control for frame injection
detection_methods: Physical layer fingerprinting detecting multiple senders for same ID
Signal plausibility checks against vehicle dynamics model

patterns.name: UDS Security Access Seed-Key Brute Force
patterns.attack_vector: Brute-forcing or reverse-engineering the UDS 0x27 security access seed-key algorithm to gain elevated diagnostic privileges
patterns.impact: Full authenticated diagnostic access to ECU, enabling firmware flashing, calibration changes, and DTC clearing
known_mitigations: Migrate from 0x27 seed-key to 0x29 certificate-based authentication
Implement attempt limiting with exponential backoff
Use server-side key derivation requiring online backend verification
patterns.description: UDS Security Access (service 0x27) uses a challenge-response mechanism where the ECU sends a seed and the tester must respond with the correct key. Many OEMs implement the key derivation as a fixed algorithm (XOR, shift, or lookup table) seeded with a constant. An attacker extracts the algorithm through firmware reverse engineering and implements it in a tool, gaining access to all ECUs of that model. Some implementations have no attempt counter, allowing unlimited brute-force attempts.
detection_methods: Failed authentication attempt counting and logging
Backend alerting on unusual diagnostic session patterns
Rate limiting on security access requests

patterns.name: UDS Diagnostic Session Hijacking
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting UDS responses to hijack an active diagnostic session between a legitimate tester and ECU
patterns.impact: Attacker takes over authenticated diagnostic session, inheriting elevated privileges without authentication
known_mitigations: Implement session binding with cryptographic tokens
Use TLS for DoIP diagnostic transport
Validate source address consistency throughout session
patterns.description: UDS diagnostic sessions on CAN have no session binding — once an extended or programming session is established, any node on the bus can send requests. An attacker monitors bus traffic for a legitimate tester opening an authenticated session, then injects their own requests using the same source address format. The ECU processes these requests within the active session context, granting the attacker access to protected services. This is particularly effective with DoIP where multiple clients can connect to the same ECU endpoint.
prerequisites: Access to same CAN bus or DoIP network as diagnostic tester
Timing alignment with legitimate session establishment
Knowledge of UDS service IDs and addressing
detection_methods: Session source address monitoring for inconsistencies
Detecting overlapping session requests from different physical sources
DoIP connection tracking with TLS client certificates

patterns.name: UDS Routine Control Exploitation
patterns.attack_vector: Invoking undocumented or manufacturer-specific UDS Routine Control (0x31) routines to access hidden ECU functionality
patterns.impact: Execution of hidden diagnostic routines enabling production-mode features, test functions, or debug capabilities in field vehicles
known_mitigations: Remove or disable development/production routines in release firmware
Require backend authorization for all routine control invocations
Implement routine access control lists per security access level
patterns.description: ECU firmware often contains diagnostic routines intended only for manufacturing or development use — actuator tests, memory read/write, sensor calibration bypass, or production end-of-line configuration. These routines are accessible via UDS Routine Control (0x31) with specific routine identifiers. An attacker who discovers these identifiers through firmware analysis can invoke them in field vehicles. Some routines bypass safety checks or enable direct actuator control that could cause physical harm if misused.
prerequisites: Firmware reverse engineering to identify routine IDs
detection_methods: Routine invocation logging with backend reporting
Whitelist of allowed routines per vehicle lifecycle state
Anomaly detection for unusual routine calls

patterns.name: DoIP Man-in-the-Middle Attack
patterns.attack_vector: ARP spoofing on workshop Ethernet to intercept DoIP diagnostic traffic between tester and vehicle gateway
patterns.impact: Complete interception and modification of diagnostic communication, enabling session hijacking and data manipulation
known_mitigations: Implement TLS 1.3 for DoIP connections per ISO 13400-2 Amendment
Use static ARP entries or 802.1X on workshop network
Implement certificate-based mutual authentication
patterns.description: Diagnostics over IP (DoIP) per ISO 13400 operates over standard Ethernet and TCP/IP. In workshop environments, the network between the diagnostic tester and the vehicle is often a simple Ethernet switch without security controls. An attacker on the same network segment performs ARP spoofing to position themselves between the tester and vehicle gateway. All diagnostic traffic flows through the attacker, who can capture security access credentials, modify diagnostic requests, or inject additional commands into the session.
prerequisites: DoIP protocol parser for real-time manipulation
detection_methods: ARP table monitoring for duplicate entries
Network traffic anomaly detection in workshop

patterns.name: OBD-II Connector Physical Attack Vector
patterns.attack_vector: Plugging malicious device into freely accessible OBD-II connector to gain persistent CAN bus access
patterns.impact: Persistent covert access to vehicle CAN bus enabling ongoing message injection, data exfiltration, or vehicle tracking
known_mitigations: Implement gateway filtering between OBD-II port and rest of vehicle network
Add authentication requirement for OBD-II access beyond emissions data
Use physical tamper detection on OBD-II connector
patterns.description: The OBD-II connector, mandated by emissions regulations in accessible locations, provides direct CAN bus access. An attacker inserts a small, concealed device (commercially available CAN interfaces with cellular or Bluetooth connectivity) into the OBD-II port. The device remains hidden under the dashboard and provides remote access to the vehicle CAN bus. Consumer-grade OBD-II dongles modified with custom firmware can perform this attack. R155 recognizes this as a threat vector since the connector cannot be removed but must be protected.
prerequisites: Brief physical access to vehicle cabin
detection_methods: Gateway detecting unexpected traffic patterns from diagnostic port
Voltage monitoring for additional load on OBD-II power pins

patterns.name: DTC Clearing for Evidence Destruction
patterns.attack_vector: Using UDS ClearDiagnosticInformation (0x14) to erase diagnostic trouble codes that record evidence of tampering or attack
patterns.impact: Forensic evidence of cyberattack or tampering destroyed, preventing investigation and accountability
known_mitigations: Log DTC clearing events to tamper-evident storage with timestamp
Require authenticated access for DTC clearing
Mirror critical DTCs to backend cloud before allowing local clear
patterns.description: After performing any attack on the vehicle, the attacker uses standard UDS service 0x14 (ClearDiagnosticInformation) to erase all diagnostic trouble codes from every ECU. Many ECUs accept this service without authentication in the default session. DTCs often contain evidence of attacks — communication errors, checksum failures, watchdog resets, or security access violations. Clearing these codes removes the forensic trail. This anti-forensics technique is trivial to execute with any commercial or open-source OBD-II diagnostic tool.
detection_methods: Backend DTC mirror detecting cleared codes
DTC clear timestamp correlation with other security events

patterns.name: UDS Firmware Readout via ReadMemoryByAddress
patterns.attack_vector: Using UDS ReadMemoryByAddress (0x23) to extract firmware from ECU memory through the diagnostic interface
patterns.impact: Firmware extracted without physical access, enabling vulnerability analysis and key extraction for the entire model fleet
known_mitigations: Restrict ReadMemoryByAddress to development builds only
Require highest security access level with backend authorization
Return encrypted memory contents that require OEM tools to decrypt
patterns.description: UDS service 0x23 (ReadMemoryByAddress) allows reading arbitrary memory regions from ECU RAM and flash. An attacker who has bypassed security access can read the entire firmware image through the diagnostic interface, block by block. This is slower than JTAG extraction but requires no physical access to the ECU PCB — only connection to the OBD-II port. The extracted firmware is then analyzed for cryptographic keys, authentication algorithms, and exploitable vulnerabilities. Some ECUs allow 0x23 access with low security access levels.
prerequisites: Security access authentication (low level may suffice)
Memory map knowledge or brute-force address scanning
detection_methods: Monitoring for sequential memory read patterns
Backend logging of security access sessions with service usage

patterns.name: Unauthorized ECU Reprogramming via Diagnostics
patterns.attack_vector: Using UDS RequestDownload (0x34) and TransferData (0x36) to flash modified firmware onto ECU through diagnostic interface
patterns.impact: Modified firmware installed on production ECU, enabling persistent backdoor, function manipulation, or safety system defeat
known_mitigations: Implement firmware signature verification in bootloader before accepting transfer
Require online backend authorization token for programming session
Use hardware anti-rollback counters verified during flashing
patterns.description: An attacker uses the standard UDS programming sequence (DiagnosticSessionControl 0x10 to programming session, SecurityAccess 0x27, RequestDownload 0x34, TransferData 0x36, RequestTransferExit 0x37) to flash modified firmware onto an ECU via the OBD-II port. If the bootloader does not verify firmware signatures or uses a weak verification scheme, the modified firmware is accepted and installed. The attacker's firmware can contain backdoors, disable safety features, or modify vehicle behavior while maintaining the appearance of normal operation.
prerequisites: Knowledge of ECU programming protocol specifics
detection_methods: Backend tracking of programming session metadata
Post-flash integrity attestation to OEM cloud

patterns.name: UDS Response Amplification Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Sending small UDS requests that generate large responses to amplify bus traffic and deny service to other ECUs
patterns.impact: CAN bus congestion from amplified diagnostic responses, causing communication delays for safety-critical messages
known_mitigations: Rate-limit diagnostic responses at gateway level
Restrict ReadDataByIdentifier to essential DIDs per session type
Implement diagnostic session timeout with automatic session end
patterns.description: An attacker sends compact UDS requests such as ReadDataByIdentifier (0x22) for DIDs that return large datasets, or ReadDTCInformation (0x19) that returns extensive freeze frame data. Each 8-byte CAN request generates multi-frame ISO-TP responses consuming significant bus bandwidth. By targeting multiple ECUs simultaneously, the attacker amplifies a small number of request frames into heavy response traffic, saturating the CAN bus and delaying legitimate high-priority messages from ADAS, braking, or steering ECUs.
prerequisites: Knowledge of DIDs that return large datasets
detection_methods: Rate-based anomaly detection on ISO-TP multi-frame sequences

patterns.name: OTA Update Package Tampering in Transit
patterns.attack_vector: Intercepting and modifying firmware update packages during download from CDN to vehicle telematics unit
patterns.impact: Modified firmware installed on vehicle ECUs, enabling persistent backdoor or safety function manipulation
known_mitigations: Cryptographic signature verification independent of transport layer
Use Ed25519 or ECDSA signatures on all update packages
Verify signature chain rooted in HSM-stored OEM CA
patterns.description: An attacker intercepts OTA update traffic between the CDN and vehicle TCU, either through DNS hijacking, rogue Wi-Fi access point, or compromised cellular infrastructure. The attacker modifies the firmware binary within the update package before forwarding it to the vehicle. If the vehicle relies solely on TLS for integrity (rather than independent cryptographic signatures), the modified firmware is accepted. R156 explicitly requires signature verification separate from transport security to prevent this class of attack.
prerequisites: Network position between CDN and vehicle
Ability to re-sign or strip signature verification
detection_methods: Package hash comparison against backend manifest

patterns.name: OTA Anti-Rollback Counter Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Manipulating software-based version counters to allow installation of older vulnerable firmware via OTA mechanism
patterns.impact: Older firmware with known vulnerabilities installed, reversing security patches across one or more ECUs
known_mitigations: Store anti-rollback counters in hardware eFuse or RPMB
Backend version tracking with minimum version enforcement
Dual verification of version at TCU and target ECU
patterns.description: An attacker exploits OTA systems that store firmware version counters in software-writable NVM rather than hardware-enforced monotonic counters. By first modifying the version counter through diagnostic access or firmware exploitation, the attacker resets the minimum accepted version. They then trigger an OTA update with an older firmware version containing known vulnerabilities. The OTA system accepts the downgrade because the manipulated counter indicates compatibility. Hardware-based eFuse counters prevent this by being physically irreversible.
prerequisites: Access to version counter storage mechanism
Understanding of OTA version validation logic

patterns.name: OTA CDN Server Compromise
patterns.attack_vector: Compromising OEM update distribution infrastructure to serve malicious firmware packages to entire vehicle fleet
patterns.impact: Fleet-wide compromise — malicious firmware distributed to all vehicles requesting updates
known_mitigations: Offline root signing key with ceremony-based access
Dual-signature requirement from independent teams
Binary transparency log for all published packages
patterns.description: An attacker compromises the OEM's software update distribution infrastructure — CDN servers, build pipeline, or package repository. By injecting malicious firmware into the distribution chain, every vehicle that checks for updates receives the compromised package. The scale of impact is fleet-wide. Mitigations require that signing keys are never accessible from the distribution infrastructure, using an air-gapped signing ceremony with multi-party authorization. Binary transparency logs provide post-compromise detection by allowing independent verification of package history.
prerequisites: Understanding of build and release pipeline

patterns.name: OTA Delta Patch Manipulation
patterns.attack_vector: Crafting malicious delta patches that produce valid post-patch hashes but contain attacker-controlled code
patterns.impact: Backdoored firmware installed through apparently valid delta update, bypassing binary-level signature checks
known_mitigations: Sign both the delta patch and the expected post-patch full image hash
Verify complete image hash after patch application before activation
Use A/B partitions to validate before switching
patterns.description: Delta OTA updates transmit only the differences between firmware versions to reduce bandwidth. An attacker who knows the current and target firmware versions crafts a malicious delta patch. When applied to the current version, this patch produces a different binary than intended but potentially passes weak verification that only checks the delta signature, not the resulting full image. Proper mitigation requires signing the expected hash of the complete post-patch image and verifying after application.
prerequisites: Knowledge of current and target firmware versions
Ability to inject modified delta into update channel

patterns.name: OTA Update Consent Mechanism Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Triggering forced firmware updates without user consent by exploiting the update scheduling mechanism
patterns.impact: Firmware updates applied while vehicle is in use, potentially causing unexpected behavior during driving
known_mitigations: Hardware interlock preventing updates when vehicle is in motion
Multi-factor consent requiring both HMI confirmation and ignition-off state
Backend rate limiting on forced update commands
patterns.description: R156 requires that OTA updates obtain user consent before installation. An attacker who gains access to the OTA management backend or spoofs update commands can trigger forced updates that bypass the consent mechanism. If the update occurs while the vehicle is in motion and causes an ECU reset or temporary function loss, it creates a safety hazard. The attack exploits the distinction between safety-critical updates (which OEMs may force-push) and normal updates (which require consent).
prerequisites: Access to OTA management backend or command channel
detection_methods: Vehicle state verification before update initiation
Update command authentication and authorization audit

patterns.name: OTA Update Metadata Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: Modifying update metadata to misdirect firmware intended for one ECU type to a different ECU
patterns.impact: Wrong firmware flashed to ECU causing malfunction, boot failure, or safety system degradation
known_mitigations: Include target ECU hardware ID in signed metadata
ECU-side hardware compatibility verification before installation
A/B partitioning with automatic rollback on boot failure
patterns.description: OTA update packages contain metadata specifying the target ECU type, hardware revision, and software version compatibility. An attacker modifies this metadata to redirect firmware meant for a non-safety ECU (e.g., infotainment) to a safety-critical ECU (e.g., braking controller). If metadata is not covered by the cryptographic signature or the ECU does not independently verify hardware compatibility, incorrect firmware is installed. The mismatched firmware causes the ECU to malfunction or brick, requiring physical repair.
prerequisites: Access to update metadata in transit or at rest
Metadata not covered by firmware signature
detection_methods: ECU hardware ID cross-check during installation
Boot failure detection and automatic rollback

patterns.name: OTA Update Campaign Replay Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Replaying a previously valid update campaign to revert vehicles to older firmware versions
patterns.impact: Vehicles reverted to older firmware, reintroducing patched vulnerabilities across targeted portion of fleet
known_mitigations: Include campaign timestamp and nonce in signed update manifest
Vehicle-side campaign ID tracking preventing re-execution
Backend campaign status tracking with vehicle acknowledgment
patterns.description: An attacker captures a legitimate OTA update campaign — including the signed manifest, firmware packages, and distribution metadata — and replays it at a later time. If the update system does not track campaign identifiers or include timestamps in the signed manifest, vehicles that have since received newer updates may process the replayed campaign and revert to the older version. This is distinct from anti-rollback bypass because it exploits the campaign management layer rather than the firmware version counter.
prerequisites: Network position to serve replayed packages

patterns.name: OTA Partial Update Interruption Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Deliberately interrupting OTA update during critical write phase to leave ECU in non-bootable state
patterns.impact: ECU bricked requiring physical dealer visit for recovery, causing fleet-wide service disruption if coordinated
known_mitigations: A/B partition scheme with atomic switchover
Failsafe bootloader with recovery mode
Power-loss-resilient update protocol with write verification
patterns.description: An attacker disrupts an OTA update during the firmware write phase by jamming cellular connectivity, triggering power interruption, or sending abort commands. ECUs without A/B partitioning that write directly to the active partition become non-bootable if the write is incomplete. A coordinated attack timing interruptions across many vehicles during a major campaign could cause widespread fleet disruption. The failsafe bootloader must be independently protected and capable of recovery or rollback.
prerequisites: Timing knowledge of active update campaigns
Ability to disrupt connectivity or power at critical moment
Target ECUs using single-partition update scheme
detection_methods: ECU heartbeat monitoring for boot failures

patterns.name: OTA TLS Certificate Pinning Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Bypassing TLS certificate pinning on vehicle TCU to intercept and modify update download traffic
patterns.impact: Update download traffic intercepted and modified, enabling firmware replacement if independent signatures are weak
known_mitigations: Implement certificate pinning in hardware-backed secure element
Use multiple independent integrity checks beyond TLS
Pin to leaf certificate, not just CA
patterns.description: The vehicle TCU pins the OEM CDN's TLS certificate to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks during update downloads. An attacker who can modify the TCU firmware or exploit a pinning implementation flaw bypasses this check, allowing traffic interception with a substitute certificate. If the OEM relies primarily on TLS for update integrity (rather than independent cryptographic signatures), the attacker can modify firmware packages in transit. This is why R156 requires signature verification independent of transport security.
prerequisites: TCU firmware access or pinning implementation vulnerability
Rogue certificate authority or self-signed certificate
detection_methods: Certificate pinning violation logging to backend
Independent signature verification at ECU level

patterns.name: OTA Selective Update Denial for Target Vehicles
patterns.attack_vector: Blocking OTA security patches from reaching specific target vehicles while allowing normal update flow to others
patterns.impact: Target vehicles remain on vulnerable firmware while fleet appears fully patched, creating persistent attack window
known_mitigations: Backend tracking of per-vehicle update status with alerting on stragglers
Vehicle-side periodic update check with randomized timing
Multiple update delivery paths (cellular, Wi-Fi, dealer)
patterns.description: An attacker selectively blocks OTA security updates from reaching specific target vehicles by interfering with their cellular connectivity, DNS resolution, or CDN access. The vehicles continue operating normally but never receive critical security patches. The OEM's fleet monitoring may not flag individual vehicles that miss updates, especially if the attacker makes them appear to check in normally. This creates a persistent vulnerability window on targeted vehicles for follow-on exploitation.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target vehicle connectivity details
Ability to selectively interfere with network access
detection_methods: Per-vehicle update status dashboard with freshness alerts

patterns.name: V2X Basic Safety Message Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: Broadcasting forged BSM (Basic Safety Message) or CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message) to nearby vehicles
patterns.impact: Receiving vehicles react to phantom vehicles — unnecessary braking, lane changes, or ADAS false activations
known_mitigations: SCMS or EU CCMS PKI-based message signing and verification
Misbehavior detection algorithms checking physical plausibility
Cross-validation with onboard sensor data (radar, camera)
patterns.description: An attacker uses a software-defined radio (SDR) or modified V2X onboard unit (OBU) to broadcast fabricated Basic Safety Messages on the 5.9 GHz DSRC band or C-V2X PC5 interface. The spoofed messages report phantom vehicles, false emergency events, or incorrect traffic signal phase information. Receiving vehicles with V2X-enabled ADAS may brake suddenly, change lanes, or modify speed in response to threats that do not exist. The SCMS PKI infrastructure mitigates this by requiring signed messages, but pre-PKI deployments or improperly validated signatures remain vulnerable.
prerequisites: SDR capable of 5.9 GHz or C-V2X transceiver
V2X message encoding knowledge (SAE J2735/ETSI ITS)

patterns.name: V2X PKI Certificate Misuse
patterns.attack_vector: Using legitimately issued but misappropriated V2X pseudonym certificates to send authenticated malicious messages
patterns.impact: Authenticated false messages accepted by all receiving vehicles, bypassing signature verification
known_mitigations: Certificate revocation via SCMS CRL distribution
Misbehavior detection triggering certificate revocation request
Short pseudonym certificate lifetimes with frequent rotation
patterns.description: An attacker extracts valid V2X pseudonym certificates from a compromised OBU or obtains certificates through a corrupted enrollment process. These certificates allow sending authenticated V2X messages that pass signature verification on all receiving vehicles. Unlike unsigned spoofing, these messages are trusted by the V2X stack. The SCMS misbehavior detection system must identify the anomalous messages and revoke the compromised certificates, but the window between misuse and revocation allows damage. Short-lived pseudonym certificates limit the useful lifetime of stolen credentials.
prerequisites: Access to valid V2X pseudonym certificates
detection_methods: Physical plausibility validation independent of PKI

patterns.name: V2X Message Replay Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Recording and replaying legitimate V2X messages in a different location or time to trigger false ADAS responses
patterns.impact: Receiving vehicles react to stale position data — phantom vehicles, false congestion, or incorrect signal phase
known_mitigations: Timestamp validation with tight freshness window
GPS-based plausibility checks on sender position
Sequence number tracking per sender pseudonym
patterns.description: An attacker records valid V2X messages from a busy intersection or highway and replays them at a different location or time. The replayed messages contain valid signatures and correct format but stale position and timestamp data. If receiving vehicles do not strictly validate message freshness or cross-check reported positions against geographic plausibility, they process the replayed messages as current. This can trigger false collision warnings, unnecessary emergency braking, or incorrect traffic signal expectations.

patterns.name: V2X Channel Flooding Denial of Service
patterns.attack_vector: Flooding the V2X communication channel with high-volume traffic to prevent legitimate message reception
patterns.impact: V2X safety messages cannot be received, disabling cooperative safety features for vehicles in the affected area
known_mitigations: Congestion control algorithms per SAE J2945/ETSI DCC
Receiver-side message prioritization and filtering
Redundant sensor coverage for safety-critical decisions
patterns.description: An attacker uses a high-power transmitter on the 5.9 GHz band to flood the V2X communication channel with noise or high-rate messages. Legitimate V2X safety messages from nearby vehicles are drowned out or delayed beyond usefulness. The DSRC/C-V2X channel capacity is limited, and congestion control mechanisms cannot distinguish malicious flooding from legitimate dense traffic. Vehicles relying on V2X for intersection safety warnings lose this capability for the duration of the attack.
detection_methods: Channel congestion monitoring beyond normal thresholds
V2X availability monitoring with fallback to sensor-only mode

patterns.name: V2X GPS Position Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: Spoofing GPS signals to make vehicles report incorrect positions in V2X messages, corrupting cooperative awareness
patterns.impact: Vehicles broadcast incorrect positions, causing other vehicles to compute wrong relative distances and collision risks
known_mitigations: Multi-constellation GNSS with spoofing detection
Inertial measurement unit cross-validation
V2X misbehavior detection flagging impossible position changes
patterns.description: An attacker uses a GPS simulator to broadcast spoofed GNSS signals stronger than real satellites. Target vehicles lock onto the spoofed signals and compute incorrect positions, which they then broadcast in V2X messages. Neighboring vehicles receive these incorrect position reports and may compute wrong time-to-collision or relative velocity values. GPS spoofing equipment is commercially available, and automotive GNSS receivers often lack spoofing detection. The compounding effect through V2X extends the attack's reach beyond the GPS spoofing range.

patterns.name: V2X Sybil Attack with Multiple Identities
patterns.attack_vector: Creating multiple fake V2X identities from single device to simulate phantom traffic congestion or incidents
patterns.impact: False congestion or incident reports cause traffic rerouting, road closures, or ADAS false activations at scale
known_mitigations: SCMS pseudonym certificate budget limiting per device
RF fingerprinting detecting multiple identities from single source
Density plausibility checks against historical traffic patterns
patterns.description: An attacker creates multiple simultaneous V2X identities from a single device, each broadcasting BSM/CAM messages reporting different positions and trajectories. This simulates a traffic jam, multi-vehicle incident, or road blockage that does not exist. Traffic management systems and individual vehicles responding to V2X congestion data reroute around the phantom obstruction. The SCMS limits pseudonym certificate allocation per device, but a compromised enrollment process or multiple compromised OBUs enables a large-scale Sybil attack.
prerequisites: Multiple valid V2X pseudonym certificate sets
V2X transmission capability with identity switching
detection_methods: Pseudonym certificate usage rate monitoring at SCMS

patterns.name: V2X Infrastructure Message Spoofing (SPaT/MAP)
patterns.attack_vector: Broadcasting fake Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT) or intersection MAP messages to mislead approaching vehicles
patterns.impact: Vehicles proceed through intersections based on false green-light information, causing potential collisions
known_mitigations: RSU certificate-based authentication for infrastructure messages
Cross-validation with camera-based traffic light recognition
Restrict safety-critical decisions to onboard sensor data only
patterns.description: Traffic signal infrastructure broadcasts SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing) and MAP (intersection geometry) messages via roadside units (RSUs). An attacker spoofs these messages with a portable transmitter near an intersection, reporting green signal phase when the actual signal is red. Connected vehicles with V2X-enabled green-light-optimal-speed-advisory (GLOSA) or automated intersection management may adjust speed or proceed based on the false signal data. The RSU certificate chain should authenticate infrastructure messages, but early deployments may lack full PKI integration.
prerequisites: RSU certificate or lack of receiver-side verification
detection_methods: Camera cross-validation of traffic signal state

patterns.name: V2X Pseudonym Linkability Tracking
patterns.attack_vector: Linking V2X pseudonym certificate rotations to track individual vehicles despite privacy protections
patterns.impact: Individual vehicle tracking despite pseudonym rotation, compromising driver privacy and enabling targeted attacks
known_mitigations: Synchronized pseudonym change with MAC address and GNSS timing randomization
Butterfly key expansion for unlinkable certificate requests
Silent period during pseudonym transitions
patterns.description: V2X systems rotate pseudonym certificates periodically to prevent vehicle tracking. However, an attacker with distributed receivers can correlate pseudonym changes by analyzing RF fingerprints, timing patterns, trajectory continuity, and non-pseudonymous identifiers (MAC address, C-V2X Layer 2 ID) that may not change simultaneously. By linking pre-change and post-change identities, the attacker reconstructs continuous vehicle trajectories. This enables surveillance, behavioral profiling, and identifying high-value targets for physical attacks.

patterns.name: V2X Misbehavior Authority Overload
patterns.attack_vector: Flooding the misbehavior authority with false misbehavior reports to overwhelm its processing and revocation capacity
patterns.impact: Misbehavior authority unable to process legitimate reports, preventing revocation of actually misbehaving vehicles
known_mitigations: Rate limiting misbehavior reports per reporting vehicle
Prioritizing reports from trusted infrastructure vs. vehicles
Distributed misbehavior detection reducing central authority load
patterns.description: The V2X Security Credential Management System (SCMS) includes a misbehavior authority that processes reports from vehicles detecting anomalous V2X messages. An attacker generates high volumes of false misbehavior reports targeting legitimate vehicles, attempting to either overwhelm the authority's processing capacity or trigger false revocations of valid certificates. If the authority cannot distinguish legitimate reports from false ones at scale, actual misbehavior goes unaddressed. This undermines the trust model that the entire V2X PKI relies on.
prerequisites: Valid V2X credentials for submitting reports
detection_methods: Report volume anomaly detection per reporter
Cross-validation of reports from independent observers

patterns.name: V2X Onboard Unit Key Extraction
patterns.attack_vector: Physical extraction of V2X enrollment credentials and private keys from vehicle onboard unit HSM
patterns.impact: Stolen V2X identity enables fully authenticated message spoofing until certificates are revoked
known_mitigations: HSM with tamper-responsive key erasure
Certificate revocation upon tamper detection
Linkage authority architecture preventing identity reconstruction from pseudonyms
patterns.description: An attacker physically extracts the V2X enrollment certificate and associated private keys from a vehicle's OBU hardware security module. Techniques include decapsulation, microprobing, or fault injection on the HSM silicon. Once extracted, the enrollment credential allows requesting fresh pseudonym certificates from the SCMS enrollment authority. The attacker can then operate a fully authenticated V2X transmitter that passes all signature checks. The SCMS must detect the anomalous enrollment behavior and revoke the compromised identity, but the window of vulnerability can be significant.
detection_methods: HSM tamper detection triggering key erasure

patterns.name: CAN Bus Message Injection
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting fabricated CAN frames to impersonate legitimate ECU and send false sensor data or control commands
patterns.impact: False sensor readings or unauthorized control commands delivered to safety-critical ECUs
known_mitigations: Implement SecOC message authentication on safety-critical CAN IDs
Deploy CAN IDS for injection detection
Use physical-layer sender identification
patterns.description: CAN bus lacks source authentication — any node can transmit any CAN ID. An attacker connects to the bus via OBD-II port, a splice, or a compromised ECU, and injects frames with CAN IDs belonging to safety-critical ECUs. For example, injecting wheel speed sensor CAN IDs with false values causes the ABS/ESC system to activate or deactivate incorrectly. The AUTOSAR SecOC standard adds MAC-based authentication to CAN frames, but retrofitting to legacy architectures is challenging due to limited payload space in classic CAN.
detection_methods: CAN IDS detecting duplicate sender for same ID

patterns.name: CAN Bus-Off Targeted ECU Silencing
patterns.attack_vector: Forcing a target ECU into CAN bus-off state by deliberately causing error frames that increment the target's error counter
patterns.impact: Target ECU permanently disconnected from CAN bus, losing all communication with vehicle network
known_mitigations: Bus-off recovery with random backoff timing
Physical-layer monitoring for error frame injection
Redundant communication paths for safety-critical ECUs
patterns.description: CAN protocol requires ECUs to track transmission errors via transmit error counters. When the counter exceeds 255, the ECU enters bus-off state and disconnects from the bus. An attacker connected to the same CAN segment transmits frames simultaneously with a target ECU, causing bit-level collisions that the target interprets as transmission errors. By repeatedly winning arbitration during the target's retransmission attempts, the attacker systematically increments the target's error counter until bus-off. This selectively silences any ECU the attacker chooses.
prerequisites: Access to same CAN bus segment as target
CAN interface with precise timing control
Knowledge of target ECU's CAN ID schedule
detection_methods: ECU bus-off event logging and alerting

patterns.name: CAN-FD Extended Payload Parsing Vulnerability
patterns.attack_vector: Sending CAN-FD frames with maximum payload (64 bytes) to ECUs with classic CAN parsers that expect 8-byte payloads
patterns.impact: Buffer overflow in CAN receive handler causing ECU crash, reset, or potential code execution
known_mitigations: Validate DLC and actual payload length in all CAN receive handlers
Enable CAN controller hardware filtering for expected DLC
Fuzz-test all ECUs with CAN-FD extended payloads
patterns.description: CAN-FD supports payloads up to 64 bytes versus classic CAN's 8 bytes. ECUs designed for classic CAN but deployed on a CAN-FD capable bus may have receive buffers and parsing routines sized for 8-byte payloads. When a CAN-FD frame arrives with 64 bytes for a CAN ID that the ECU processes, the parser writes beyond the receive buffer boundary. This buffer overflow can crash the ECU or, in specific memory layouts, enable controlled code execution. Mixed classic/FD deployments are particularly vulnerable during architecture transitions.
prerequisites: Target ECU on same bus segment
Knowledge of ECU CAN ID acceptance filter
detection_methods: ECU crash and reset pattern detection
Receive buffer bounds checking in code review

patterns.name: Automotive Ethernet ARP Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: ARP cache poisoning on automotive Ethernet to intercept or redirect traffic between ECUs
patterns.impact: Traffic interception between ECUs, enabling eavesdropping on SOME/IP communications or message modification
known_mitigations: Use static ARP tables for safety-critical ECU communication
Implement MACsec on all automotive Ethernet links
Deploy ARP inspection on automotive Ethernet switches
patterns.description: Automotive Ethernet networks use standard ARP for address resolution. An attacker connected to the Ethernet segment sends gratuitous ARP replies associating their MAC address with the IP of a target ECU. Other ECUs update their ARP caches and send traffic intended for the target to the attacker instead. The attacker forwards the traffic to the actual destination after inspection or modification, maintaining connectivity while intercepting all communication. This is a standard IT network attack directly applicable to automotive Ethernet.
prerequisites: Standard Ethernet interface and ARP spoofing tools
Knowledge of ECU IP address assignments

patterns.name: Automotive Ethernet MACsec Downgrade Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting MACsec capability negotiation to force ECU communication to fall back to unprotected Ethernet
patterns.impact: Link-layer encryption disabled, exposing all traffic on the Ethernet link to eavesdropping and manipulation
known_mitigations: Configure MACsec as mandatory, not optional with fallback
Implement MKA with pre-shared keys or 802.1X without downgrade options
Monitor for MACsec session teardown events
patterns.description: MACsec provides link-layer encryption and authentication for automotive Ethernet. During the MACsec Key Agreement (MKA) protocol, two ECUs negotiate security parameters. An attacker on the link exploits optional MACsec configurations that allow fallback to unprotected communication if MKA fails. By disrupting the MKA handshake through frame injection or timing manipulation, the attacker forces both endpoints to communicate without MACsec protection. This exposes all Ethernet traffic to eavesdropping, which is especially damaging for ADAS camera and sensor data streams.
prerequisites: Physical access to Ethernet link between ECUs
Target ECUs configured with MACsec fallback option

patterns.name: SecOC Freshness Value Replay Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Replaying authenticated CAN messages by exploiting weak freshness value implementations in SecOC
patterns.impact: Old authenticated messages accepted as current, enabling replay of valid commands or sensor data
known_mitigations: Use monotonically increasing freshness counters with sufficient bit width
Implement trip-based freshness reset with synchronized counter initialization
Deploy freshness value manager with adequate counter distribution
patterns.description: SecOC authenticates CAN messages using truncated MACs with freshness values to prevent replay attacks. However, the freshness counter bit width on CAN is limited (typically 4-8 bits transmitted with 24+ bits managed by the freshness value manager). An attacker who captures authenticated messages and understands the freshness counter scheme can replay messages when the counter wraps around or after a freshness value reset event (e.g., ECU power cycle). Weak implementations with small counters or predictable reset behavior are vulnerable.
prerequisites: Understanding of counter wrap-around or reset conditions
detection_methods: Duplicate message detection within counter window

patterns.name: SOME/IP Deserialization Vulnerability Exploitation
patterns.attack_vector: Sending malformed SOME/IP payloads to exploit deserialization bugs in receiving ECU service implementations
patterns.impact: Remote code execution or denial of service on ECU processing SOME/IP service requests
known_mitigations: Implement strict input validation on all SOME/IP method handlers
Use generated serialization code with bounds checking from ARXML
Fuzz-test all SOME/IP interfaces during development
patterns.description: SOME/IP uses complex serialization for structured data types in automotive service-oriented communication. ECU service implementations that manually parse SOME/IP payloads may contain deserialization vulnerabilities — integer overflows in length fields, out-of-bounds access on variable-length arrays, or type confusion in union types. An attacker sends crafted SOME/IP method requests with malformed payloads designed to trigger these parsing bugs. Since SOME/IP runs over UDP or TCP, exploitation can be achieved from any node on the automotive Ethernet network.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target SOME/IP service interface definition
detection_methods: Service crash detection and restart monitoring

patterns.name: CAN ISO-TP Fragmentation Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting ISO 15765-2 transport protocol fragmentation handling to inject data or cause denial of service
patterns.impact: Corrupted multi-frame messages delivered to application layer or ECU transport layer crash
known_mitigations: Implement strict ISO-TP state machine validation
Validate sequence numbers and length consistency in consecutive frames
Apply timeouts for incomplete multi-frame transfers
patterns.description: ISO-TP (ISO 15765-2) provides transport layer segmentation for CAN, splitting large messages into first frames, consecutive frames, and flow control. An attacker injects forged consecutive frames into an ongoing legitimate multi-frame transfer, corrupting the reassembled message at the receiving ECU. Alternatively, the attacker sends first frames with extremely large length values to exhaust receive buffers, or sends flow control frames with zero block size and minimum separation time to overwhelm the sender. These attacks exploit the lack of authentication at the transport layer.
prerequisites: Access to CAN bus during active ISO-TP transfers
Knowledge of active CAN ID pairs using ISO-TP

patterns.name: Automotive Ethernet AVTP Stream Injection
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting false IEEE 1722 (AVTP) media streams to deliver manipulated camera or LiDAR data to ADAS ECU
patterns.impact: ADAS ECU receives manipulated sensor data — false objects, removed obstacles, or shifted lane markings
known_mitigations: Implement stream authentication for AVTP using IEEE 1722 security extensions
Cross-validate AVTP streams against independent sensor modalities
Use MACsec on links carrying AVTP traffic
patterns.description: IEEE 1722 Audio/Video Transport Protocol (AVTP) carries camera, LiDAR, and radar data streams over automotive Ethernet in ADAS architectures. An attacker on the Ethernet network injects a forged AVTP stream with the same stream ID as a legitimate camera feed. The ADAS ECU receives frames from both the legitimate and forged streams, potentially processing the attacker's data. By injecting modified video frames that remove pedestrians or add phantom obstacles, the attacker can manipulate the ADAS perception pipeline. Stream-level authentication is not yet widely deployed.
prerequisites: Access to automotive Ethernet carrying AVTP streams
Knowledge of AVTP stream IDs and format
detection_methods: Stream timing and sequence anomaly detection

patterns.name: CAN Network Topology Discovery and Mapping
patterns.attack_vector: Passive eavesdropping on CAN bus to map all active ECUs, their CAN IDs, and message timing for attack planning
patterns.impact: Complete vehicle network map obtained — CAN IDs, timing, payloads, and ECU relationships documented for further attacks
known_mitigations: Encrypt CAN payloads for sensitive signals
Implement bus access authentication
Deploy IDS that detects passive monitoring devices via physical layer anomalies
patterns.description: CAN bus is a broadcast medium — every node receives every frame. An attacker connects a passive listener to the CAN bus (via OBD-II or physical tap) and records all traffic. By analyzing CAN IDs, data patterns, timing, and correlating with known DBC database structures, the attacker builds a complete map of the vehicle network. This map identifies which ECUs are present, what signals they send, their transmission cycles, and how signals relate to physical vehicle behavior. This reconnaissance enables targeted follow-on attacks against specific ECUs or functions.
detection_methods: Physical-layer detection of additional bus loading
Voltage level anomaly from added transceiver

patterns.name: TCU Cellular Man-in-the-Middle via Rogue Base Station
patterns.attack_vector: Deploying rogue cellular base station (IMSI catcher) to intercept TCU communication with OEM backend
patterns.impact: All TCU backend communication intercepted — OTA commands, telemetry data, remote control signals exposed
known_mitigations: Implement application-layer encryption independent of cellular transport
Use certificate pinning for backend TLS connections
Deploy rogue base station detection in TCU modem firmware
patterns.description: An attacker deploys a rogue LTE/5G base station (IMSI catcher) near target vehicles. The vehicle's TCU cellular modem connects to the stronger rogue signal, routing all backend communication through the attacker. If the TCU relies solely on cellular-layer encryption (which the rogue base station terminates), all traffic is exposed. The attacker can intercept OTA update commands, vehicle telemetry, remote lock/unlock signals, and geolocation data. Application-layer TLS with certificate pinning mitigates this, but many TCU implementations trust any valid TLS certificate from the cellular network.
prerequisites: Knowledge of TCU APN and backend endpoints

patterns.name: TCU Remote Command Injection via Backend API
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting vulnerabilities in OEM backend API to send unauthorized remote commands to vehicle TCU
patterns.impact: Unauthorized remote vehicle control — door unlock, engine start, horn, or HVAC manipulation at scale
known_mitigations: Implement per-vehicle command authorization with owner confirmation
Use signed command tokens with short expiry and VIN binding
Rate limit and anomaly detect backend-to-vehicle command volume
patterns.description: OEM backend APIs provide remote vehicle management — lock/unlock, engine start, climate control, and diagnostics. An attacker who compromises the backend API (through credential theft, API vulnerability, or insider access) can send commands to any vehicle in the fleet. Many implementations use session tokens or API keys without per-command vehicle owner verification. A compromised API key enables mass vehicle manipulation. The attack scales because a single backend vulnerability affects every connected vehicle, not just one.
prerequisites: Compromised backend API credentials or API vulnerability
Knowledge of command API format and vehicle VINs

patterns.name: TCU SIM Card Cloning or Swap
patterns.attack_vector: Cloning or swapping the TCU's embedded SIM to impersonate the vehicle on the cellular network
patterns.impact: Attacker impersonates vehicle to OEM backend, receiving commands and data intended for the real vehicle
known_mitigations: Use eSIM with remote provisioning and tamper detection
Implement device attestation beyond SIM identity
Bind TCU identity to HSM-backed device certificate independent of SIM
patterns.description: An attacker with physical access to the vehicle extracts the TCU SIM card or clones its credentials. Using the cloned SIM in a controlled device, the attacker connects to the cellular network as the vehicle's TCU. The OEM backend cannot distinguish the real TCU from the clone. The attacker receives OTA updates, remote commands, and configuration data intended for the vehicle. Additionally, the attacker can send spoofed telemetry data to the backend, corrupting fleet analytics or triggering false maintenance alerts.
prerequisites: Physical access to TCU for SIM extraction
SIM cloning equipment or social engineering for SIM swap
detection_methods: Dual registration detection by cellular operator
Device attestation using HSM beyond SIM identity

patterns.name: TCU Bluetooth Stack Remote Code Execution
patterns.attack_vector: Exploiting Bluetooth Classic or BLE stack vulnerabilities on TCU/IVI to gain remote code execution
patterns.impact: Remote code execution on TCU or IVI unit, providing foothold for lateral movement to vehicle CAN bus
known_mitigations: Keep Bluetooth stack firmware updated with security patches
Disable unused Bluetooth profiles
Isolate Bluetooth-connected systems from safety-critical bus segments via gateway
patterns.description: Bluetooth stacks in automotive TCUs and infotainment systems have historically contained critical vulnerabilities (BlueBorne, BrakTooth, BLUFFS). An attacker within Bluetooth range (typically 10-100m) exploits these vulnerabilities to achieve remote code execution on the TCU or IVI processor. From this initial foothold, the attacker pivots to access the vehicle CAN bus through the TCU's diagnostic or gateway interface. The attack requires no user interaction — the Bluetooth stack processes pairing and discovery packets before authentication.
prerequisites: Bluetooth radio within range of target vehicle
Knowledge of TCU Bluetooth stack version
detection_methods: Anomalous CAN traffic from TCU after Bluetooth connection

patterns.name: TCU Wi-Fi Evil Twin Hotspot Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Setting up rogue Wi-Fi access point matching vehicle's known network SSID to intercept TCU Wi-Fi traffic
patterns.impact: TCU connects to attacker's network, exposing backend communication and enabling traffic manipulation
known_mitigations: Use 802.1X enterprise authentication for TCU Wi-Fi connections
Implement certificate pinning for all backend TLS connections
Disable auto-connect for non-verified networks
patterns.description: Vehicle TCUs with Wi-Fi capability often auto-connect to known SSIDs for faster data transfer (e.g., dealer workshop Wi-Fi for OTA updates). An attacker broadcasts a rogue access point with the same SSID and stronger signal. The TCU connects automatically, routing all traffic through the attacker. If the TCU does not implement certificate pinning or 802.1X authentication, the attacker can intercept and modify backend communication. At dealer workshops where vehicles routinely connect to Wi-Fi, this attack is particularly effective.
prerequisites: Knowledge of SSID the vehicle auto-connects to
Rogue AP hardware with adequate signal strength
detection_methods: Wi-Fi BSSID verification against known list

patterns.name: Vehicle Telemetry Data Exfiltration via TCU
patterns.attack_vector: Compromising TCU to silently exfiltrate vehicle location, driver behavior, and occupant data to attacker-controlled server
patterns.impact: Continuous surveillance of vehicle location, routes, driving patterns, and potentially audio from cabin microphone
known_mitigations: Network egress filtering on TCU restricting connections to whitelisted OEM endpoints
Traffic volume monitoring for anomalous outbound data
Encrypt and authenticate all CAN-to-TCU data paths
patterns.description: An attacker who has gained code execution on the TCU (via Bluetooth, cellular, or Wi-Fi exploit) installs persistent malware that collects vehicle telemetry. The compromised TCU reads GPS position, speed, accelerometer data, CAN bus signals, and potentially cabin microphone audio. The data is exfiltrated over the cellular connection to an attacker-controlled server, using encrypted channels that appear as normal TCU backend traffic. Because the TCU has legitimate network connectivity, the exfiltration traffic blends with normal telemetry uploads.
prerequisites: Understanding of TCU OS and data access APIs
detection_methods: Outbound connection monitoring for non-OEM destinations
Data volume anomaly detection on cellular interface

patterns.name: TCU Firmware Modification via Compromised OTA Channel
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting malicious TCU firmware update through compromised cellular or Wi-Fi OTA channel
patterns.impact: Persistent TCU compromise surviving reboots, enabling long-term vehicle surveillance and control relay
known_mitigations: TCU secure boot with hardware root of trust
OTA update signature verification using HSM-stored keys
Verified boot chain reporting status to OEM backend
patterns.description: An attacker who controls the TCU's network path (via rogue base station or Wi-Fi evil twin) serves a malicious firmware update to the TCU. If the TCU lacks secure boot with hardware-backed signature verification, the modified firmware is installed persistently. The attacker's firmware maintains all legitimate TCU functionality while adding backdoor access, data exfiltration, and the ability to relay commands to the vehicle CAN bus. This persistence survives vehicle power cycles and standard troubleshooting.
prerequisites: Network interception position for TCU communications
detection_methods: Firmware hash mismatch with OEM golden image

patterns.name: TCU/IVI USB Interface Exploitation
patterns.attack_vector: Connecting malicious USB device to vehicle IVI/TCU USB port to exploit USB stack vulnerabilities
patterns.impact: Code execution on IVI/TCU processor via USB stack exploit, enabling CAN bus access from cabin
known_mitigations: Implement USB device class whitelisting (allow only mass storage, audio)
Run USB drivers in sandboxed context
Disable USB debug and ADB interfaces in production firmware
patterns.description: Vehicle infotainment and telematics units expose USB ports for media playback, phone connectivity, and software updates. An attacker connects a USB device that exploits vulnerabilities in the USB host stack — BadUSB attacks impersonating keyboards for command injection, malformed USB descriptors triggering buffer overflows in driver parsing, or USB firmware update abuse. Many IVI systems run Android Automotive or Linux with standard USB stacks that inherit IT-world USB vulnerabilities. Physical cabin access is the only requirement.
prerequisites: Physical access to vehicle USB port
Malicious USB device prepared for target IVI/TCU platform
Knowledge of target OS and USB stack version
detection_methods: USB device class and descriptor validation
Process monitoring for unexpected child processes after USB connect

patterns.name: TCU GNSS Signal Jamming
