patterns.attack_vector: Broadcasting radio interference on GNSS frequencies to deny vehicle position accuracy
patterns.impact: Vehicle loses GPS position fix — navigation fails, geofencing disabled, fleet tracking blinded
known_mitigations: Multi-constellation GNSS receiver with jamming detection
Dead reckoning via IMU and wheel speed sensors during GNSS denial
Report GNSS quality metrics to backend for fleet-level anomaly detection
patterns.description: An attacker uses a low-cost GNSS jammer (commercially available despite illegality) to deny GPS service to nearby vehicles. The TCU loses its position fix, breaking navigation, geofencing, fleet tracking, and location-dependent services. GNSS jamming devices are small enough to be carried in a pocket and affect vehicles within tens to hundreds of meters. For fleet vehicles, this disrupts logistics operations. For personal vehicles, it can disable anti-theft tracking. The attack requires no technical skill — just turning on the jammer.

patterns.name: TCU MQTT Broker Credential Theft and Abuse
patterns.attack_vector: Extracting MQTT broker credentials from TCU firmware to subscribe to or publish on vehicle telemetry topics
patterns.impact: Access to real-time telemetry for entire fleet, ability to publish false data or commands via MQTT topics
known_mitigations: Use per-vehicle unique MQTT credentials derived from HSM-stored identity
Implement MQTT topic-level ACLs restricting per-vehicle access scope
Rotate MQTT credentials periodically with certificate-based authentication
patterns.description: Many connected vehicle platforms use MQTT for telemetry data transport between vehicle TCUs and cloud backend. The MQTT broker credentials are embedded in TCU firmware — if all vehicles use the same credentials or the credentials are derived from extractable parameters (like VIN), an attacker who extracts them from one vehicle can access the broker. With broker access, the attacker subscribes to telemetry topics for any vehicle, receiving real-time location, speed, and diagnostic data for the entire fleet. If the broker lacks topic-level ACLs, the attacker can also publish commands.
prerequisites: MQTT credential extraction from firmware image
Network access to MQTT broker endpoint

patterns.name: LiDAR Sensor Spoofing via Laser Injection
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting laser pulses synchronized with LiDAR scan pattern to create phantom objects in point cloud
patterns.impact: ADAS perceives phantom obstacles causing unnecessary emergency braking or evasive maneuvers
known_mitigations: Randomize LiDAR scan timing and encoding
Cross-validate LiDAR detections with radar and camera
Implement LiDAR pulse fingerprinting for authenticity verification
patterns.description: An attacker uses a pulsed laser synchronized with the target vehicle's LiDAR scan pattern to inject false return signals. The LiDAR sensor interprets these injected pulses as reflections from objects at specific distances. By controlling the timing and intensity, the attacker creates phantom objects (walls, vehicles, pedestrians) in the LiDAR point cloud. ADAS systems processing this corrupted point cloud may trigger emergency braking or swerving. The attack requires line-of-sight to the LiDAR sensor and knowledge of its operating frequency and scan pattern.
prerequisites: Pulsed laser matching target LiDAR wavelength
detection_methods: Scan pattern randomization detecting synchronized injection

patterns.name: Camera Adversarial Patch Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Placing physical adversarial patches in the environment that cause DNN-based classifiers to misidentify objects
patterns.impact: Object classification errors — stop signs misread, pedestrians undetected, or lane markings misinterpreted
known_mitigations: Adversarial training with patch augmentation in DNN training pipeline
Multi-modal verification requiring camera agreement with radar detections
Input preprocessing with spatial smoothing to neutralize high-frequency patches
patterns.description: An attacker prints and places adversarial patches on road infrastructure or vehicles. These patches contain patterns specifically crafted to cause deep neural network (DNN) classifiers to misidentify objects — a stop sign classified as a speed limit sign, a pedestrian classified as background, or lane markings interpreted incorrectly. The patches exploit known vulnerabilities in DNN architectures where small, targeted perturbations cause confident misclassification. Research has demonstrated physical-world effectiveness of these attacks under varying lighting and angle conditions.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target DNN architecture or transferable adversarial examples
Physical access to place patches in target area
detection_methods: Adversarial example detection modules in perception pipeline
Temporal consistency checking across consecutive frames

patterns.name: Automotive Radar Jamming Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Broadcasting RF interference on 77 GHz radar band to blind vehicle radar sensors
patterns.impact: Radar-based ADAS features disabled — adaptive cruise control, collision avoidance, and blind spot detection fail
known_mitigations: Implement radar interference detection and reporting
Design ADAS to degrade gracefully with driver notification on sensor loss
Use frequency hopping or spread-spectrum techniques in radar waveform
patterns.description: An attacker transmits interference signals on the 76-81 GHz automotive radar band. The interference overwhelms the radar receiver, preventing detection of real targets. This blinds ADAS features dependent on radar — adaptive cruise control loses track of the lead vehicle, automatic emergency braking cannot detect obstacles, and blind-spot monitoring fails. Unlike LiDAR spoofing which creates phantom objects, radar jamming causes absence of real objects from the perception. The vehicle may continue driving without radar input if the ADAS does not properly handle sensor degradation.
prerequisites: 77 GHz RF signal generator or radar jammer
Proximity to target vehicle (within radar operating range)
detection_methods: Sudden target loss correlation across multiple radar channels
Driver alert on radar performance degradation

patterns.name: Automotive Radar Target Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: Retransmitting radar signals with modified delay to create phantom targets at controlled distances
patterns.impact: Radar reports phantom vehicles or objects at attacker-chosen distances, triggering ADAS false activations
known_mitigations: Implement radar waveform authentication or randomization
Cross-validate radar targets with camera and LiDAR
Apply Doppler consistency checks for phantom target detection
patterns.description: An attacker captures the vehicle's transmitted FMCW radar chirp, applies a controlled time delay, and retransmits it toward the radar receiver. The radar interprets the delayed reflection as a target at a specific range proportional to the added delay. By varying the delay, the attacker controls the apparent distance of the phantom target. Advanced attacks also shift the frequency to spoof Doppler velocity. The vehicle's ADAS reacts to the phantom target — braking, accelerating, or changing lanes based on the spoofed radar data.
prerequisites: Radar front-end capable of receive and retransmit at 77 GHz
Synchronization with target vehicle's radar chirp

patterns.name: Ultrasonic Parking Sensor Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: Generating ultrasonic signals to create phantom obstacles or mask real objects in parking sensor coverage
patterns.impact: Parking assist system reports false obstacles or fails to detect real ones, causing collision or usability issues
known_mitigations: Cross-validate ultrasonic readings with camera-based parking view
Implement ultrasonic pulse coding for authentication
Use multiple sensor fusion for parking assist decisions
patterns.description: Ultrasonic parking sensors operate on simple time-of-flight measurement with no signal authentication. An attacker uses an ultrasonic transducer to inject signals that the parking sensor interprets as reflections from objects. By controlling timing, the attacker creates phantom obstacles preventing the driver from parking in a particular spot, or conversely, saturates the sensor with constant signals that mask real obstacles behind the vehicle. Consumer-grade ultrasonic transducers and signal generators are sufficient for this attack.
prerequisites: Ultrasonic transducer matching sensor frequency (40-48 kHz typical)
Signal generator or microcontroller for timing control
detection_methods: Temporal consistency checks across sensor array

patterns.name: Camera Sensor Blinding via Directed Light
patterns.attack_vector: Directing high-intensity light at vehicle camera sensors to cause image saturation and blindness
patterns.impact: Camera-based ADAS features disabled — lane keeping, traffic sign recognition, and pedestrian detection fail
known_mitigations: Implement auto-exposure and HDR imaging to handle glare
Cross-validate camera data with radar and LiDAR
Detect and report camera blinding events as sensor degradation
patterns.description: An attacker directs a high-intensity light source (laser pointer, LED array, or reflected sunlight) at the vehicle's front-facing camera. The camera sensor saturates, producing whiteout images that the perception algorithms cannot process. Lane markings, traffic signs, pedestrians, and other vehicles become invisible to the camera system. If the ADAS relies primarily on camera for these functions (as in vision-centric architectures), the vehicle loses critical safety features. Unlike jamming, camera blinding is visible to the driver but may not trigger automatic system degradation.

patterns.name: IMU Acoustic Resonance Injection Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Using targeted acoustic signals at MEMS resonant frequency to inject false readings into inertial measurement unit
patterns.impact: False acceleration or rotation data injected, causing stability control errors or dead reckoning drift
known_mitigations: Acoustic shielding of MEMS IMU package
Low-pass filtering to remove resonant frequency components
Cross-validate IMU with wheel speed sensors and GPS
patterns.description: MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes have mechanical resonant frequencies typically between 5-30 kHz. An attacker generates acoustic signals at these frequencies using a speaker or piezoelectric transducer. The acoustic energy causes the MEMS proof mass to vibrate at the resonant frequency, injecting false acceleration or rotation signals into the IMU output. This corrupts the vehicle stability control, rollover detection, and dead reckoning navigation. Research has demonstrated this attack against automotive-grade MEMS sensors at distances of several meters.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target IMU MEMS resonant frequency
Acoustic transducer capable of required frequency and power
detection_methods: Resonant frequency bandpass detection on IMU output

patterns.name: Wheel Speed Sensor Signal Manipulation
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting false signals into wheel speed sensor wiring to corrupt ABS/ESC input data
patterns.impact: ABS and ESC compute incorrect wheel speeds — braking force distribution errors, false stability interventions
known_mitigations: Use active wheel speed sensors with encrypted digital output
Implement plausibility checks comparing all four wheel speeds
Physical tamper detection on sensor wiring harness
patterns.description: Passive wheel speed sensors generate simple AC signals proportional to wheel rotation speed. An attacker with physical access to the wheel well or underbody connects to the sensor wiring and injects additional AC signals. The receiving ECU (ABS/ESC controller) sums the real and injected signals, computing incorrect wheel speed. This causes the ABS to apply incorrect braking force distribution or the ESC to intervene unnecessarily. Active sensors with digital protocols are more resistant, but many vehicles still use passive magnetic sensors on rear axles.
prerequisites: Physical access to wheel speed sensor wiring
Signal generator matching sensor output characteristics
Knowledge of sensor type (passive Hall or active)

patterns.name: Camera Data Stream Frame Injection via Ethernet
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting modified camera frames into automotive Ethernet data stream between camera module and ADAS ECU
patterns.impact: ADAS processes manipulated camera images — objects removed, phantom objects added, or scene content altered
known_mitigations: Authenticate camera data streams at the link and transport layer
Use MACsec on camera Ethernet connections
Implement frame sequence numbering with cryptographic integrity
patterns.description: In camera-over-Ethernet architectures, raw or compressed camera frames travel from the camera module to the ADAS ECU over automotive Ethernet. An attacker who gains access to this Ethernet segment (through physical tap or compromised switch) can inject modified frames into the stream. By replacing legitimate frames with altered versions — removing pedestrians, shifting lane markings, or adding phantom objects — the attacker manipulates the ADAS perception input. Frame-level authentication using MACsec or application-layer signing prevents this but adds latency.
prerequisites: Physical access to camera Ethernet link
Knowledge of camera stream protocol and format
detection_methods: Temporal consistency detection across consecutive frames

patterns.name: GPS Spoofing for Navigation Manipulation
patterns.attack_vector: Transmitting counterfeit GPS signals to gradually shift vehicle's perceived position and mislead navigation
patterns.impact: Vehicle navigation misled — driver routed to wrong location, geofencing boundaries shifted, fleet tracking corrupted
known_mitigations: Multi-constellation GNSS with cross-constellation consistency checks
GPS spoofing detection using signal characteristics analysis
IMU-based dead reckoning cross-validation with GPS position
patterns.description: An attacker uses a GPS simulator to broadcast counterfeit GPS signals that gradually shift the vehicle's computed position. Unlike jamming, spoofing is stealthy — the vehicle continues reporting a position, but the position is incorrect. The attacker can redirect navigation to wrong locations, shift geofencing boundaries to allow vehicles into restricted areas, or corrupt fleet tracking data. The gradual position shift avoids triggering sudden-jump detection. Autonomous vehicles relying on GPS for localization are particularly at risk for route manipulation.
prerequisites: GPS signal simulator capable of multi-satellite spoofing
Proximity to or following target vehicle
Knowledge of target area for plausible position shift

patterns.name: Keyless Entry Relay Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Relaying RF signal between key fob and vehicle using two coordinated devices to extend communication range
patterns.impact: Vehicle unlocked and engine started without owner's knowledge, enabling theft
known_mitigations: Implement UWB distance bounding for precise proximity verification
Add motion sensor to key fob disabling relay when stationary
Use BLE-based proximity detection as secondary check
patterns.description: Passive keyless entry systems (PKES) use low-frequency challenge and UHF response between vehicle and key fob, assuming proximity means authorization. Two attackers with relay devices extend this communication over arbitrary distances — one device near the vehicle captures the LF challenge, relays it to the second device near the owner's key fob (e.g., through the front door of their house), which triggers the fob's UHF response relayed back to the vehicle. The vehicle and fob both believe they are in close proximity. This is the most common method of keyless vehicle theft.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target vehicle's key fob proximity
detection_methods: Vehicle anomaly detection for unlock without key movement

patterns.name: Key Fob Rolling Code Prediction
patterns.attack_vector: Predicting or exploiting weaknesses in RKE rolling code PRNG to generate valid future codes
patterns.impact: Attacker can unlock and potentially start vehicle at any time using predicted valid codes
known_mitigations: Use cryptographically strong PRNG for rolling code generation
Implement AES-based challenge-response instead of rolling codes
Limit rolling code window to reject codes too far ahead
patterns.description: RKE (Remote Keyless Entry) systems use rolling codes where each button press generates the next code in a PRNG sequence. If the PRNG algorithm is weak or uses insufficient key length, an attacker who captures several consecutive codes can reverse-engineer the PRNG state and predict all future codes. Historical examples include KeeLoq (broken via side-channel and algebraic attacks) and Hitag2 (broken via correlation attacks). Once the PRNG state is known, the attacker generates valid codes indefinitely without needing the original key fob.
prerequisites: RF receiver to capture rolling codes
Sufficient captured codes for PRNG state recovery
detection_methods: Backend tracking of lock/unlock events vs. owner app location

patterns.name: CAN Bus Injection Vehicle Unlock
patterns.attack_vector: Injecting unlock command CAN frames directly on the bus to bypass external RF access control entirely
patterns.impact: Vehicle doors unlocked, potentially engine started, by bypassing all RF-layer access control
known_mitigations: Authenticate door lock CAN commands with SecOC
Restrict CAN IDs for lock/unlock to body controller only
Gateway filtering blocking lock commands from unauthorized bus segments
patterns.description: Once an attacker has any access to the vehicle CAN bus (via OBD-II dongle, compromised ECU, or physical wire tap), they can inject the CAN frames that the body control module uses to lock and unlock doors. These frames are typically unauthenticated in vehicles without SecOC. The attacker bypasses all RF-level access control (key fob, NFC, phone key) by commanding the body controller directly at the CAN protocol level. Combined with engine start CAN commands, this enables full vehicle theft through the diagnostic or internal network port.
prerequisites: CAN bus access via any method
Knowledge of door lock/unlock CAN IDs and payloads
detection_methods: SecOC authentication of lock/unlock CAN commands
Gateway filtering preventing lock commands from diagnostic port
Anomalous lock/unlock event logging and alerting

patterns.name: Phone-as-Key Bluetooth Relay and Exploit
patterns.attack_vector: Relaying or exploiting BLE phone-as-key communication to unlock vehicle without authorized phone present
patterns.impact: Vehicle unlocked and started using relayed phone-as-key signal, enabling theft without physical key or phone
known_mitigations: Implement UWB ranging for BLE phone-as-key
Require user confirmation on phone app for unlock
Use BLE channel hopping analysis for relay detection
patterns.description: Phone-as-key systems replace traditional key fobs with smartphone BLE communication. These systems inherit relay attack vulnerability from PKES — an attacker relays the BLE signal between the owner's phone and the vehicle using two COTS Bluetooth-capable devices. BLE relay attacks have lower latency requirements than LF/UHF relay, and BLE signal processing adds relay-detection-defeating delays. Some implementations also have app-level vulnerabilities (insecure token storage, API bypasses) that allow key cloning without physical relay.
prerequisites: BLE relay devices or phone-as-key app vulnerability
Two coordinated attackers for relay scenario
Knowledge of target vehicle BLE protocol
detection_methods: BLE round-trip time analysis for relay detection
Phone app location vs. vehicle location comparison

patterns.name: NFC Key Card Cloning Attack
patterns.attack_vector: Reading and cloning NFC key card credentials using portable NFC reader to create duplicate access card
patterns.impact: Cloned NFC key provides persistent vehicle access identical to legitimate key card
known_mitigations: Use NFC cards with cryptographic challenge-response (e.g., DESFire EV3)
Implement per-session random challenges that cannot be replayed
Backend-track NFC card usage for duplicate detection
patterns.description: Some vehicles use NFC cards as backup access method. An attacker with a portable NFC reader (concealed as a phone or wallet) approaches the vehicle owner in a public setting and reads the NFC card credentials through clothing or bag at ranges up to 10 cm. If the NFC card uses static credentials (UID-only authentication or weak crypto like MIFARE Classic), the attacker writes these credentials to a blank card. The cloned card unlocks the vehicle identically to the original. Cards with strong cryptographic authentication prevent cloning but add cost and complexity.
prerequisites: Brief proximity to owner's NFC key card
Blank writable NFC card for cloning

patterns.name: Engine Immobilizer Transponder Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Bypassing immobilizer transponder authentication using emulation device or ECU reprogramming
patterns.impact: Engine starts without authorized transponder — immobilizer security completely defeated
known_mitigations: Use AES-128 or stronger transponder authentication
Bind immobilizer to ECU via HSM with anti-tamper
Implement immobilizer bypass attempt logging to backend
patterns.description: The engine immobilizer uses an RFID transponder in the key to authenticate with the engine ECU before allowing ignition. An attacker defeats this through several methods: reprogramming the engine ECU to accept new transponder keys (using stolen dealer tools or extracted seed-key algorithms), emulating the transponder response using captured challenge-response pairs, or exploiting weak cryptography in legacy transponder protocols (e.g., Hitag2, DST40). Aftermarket immobilizer bypass devices are commercially available for many vehicle models.
prerequisites: Transponder emulator or ECU programming tool
Physical access to vehicle ignition system

patterns.name: Key Fob Signal Jamming for Theft Preparation
patterns.attack_vector: Jamming key fob lock signal so vehicle remains unlocked while owner believes it is secured
patterns.impact: Vehicle left unlocked in parking lot without owner awareness, enabling opportunistic theft of contents or vehicle
known_mitigations: Visual/audible lock confirmation feedback independent of RF
Vehicle auto-lock after timeout if no motion detected
Lock state verification via companion app notification
patterns.description: An attacker positioned in a parking area operates a broadband RF jammer on the 315/433 MHz or 868 MHz frequencies used by key fobs. When the owner presses the lock button, the RF signal is jammed and never reaches the vehicle receiver. The vehicle remains unlocked, but the owner walks away believing it is locked. The attacker then accesses the vehicle at leisure. This is a low-skill, high-success-rate attack because many owners do not verify lock status visually. Some vehicles provide visual confirmation (mirror fold, indicator flash) that would alert the owner.
prerequisites: Broadband RF jammer covering key fob frequencies
Positioning in parking area near target vehicle
detection_methods: Vehicle auto-lock timeout when driver walks away
RF interference detection at vehicle receiver

patterns.name: Backend Digital Key Provisioning Compromise
patterns.attack_vector: Attacking OEM backend system to provision unauthorized digital keys for target vehicles
patterns.impact: Unauthorized digital keys provisioned for any vehicle in fleet, enabling remote or proximity-based access
known_mitigations: Multi-factor owner verification for digital key provisioning
Rate limiting and anomaly detection on key provisioning API
Vehicle-side owner confirmation for new key acceptance
patterns.description: Digital key systems (CCC, ICCE) allow OEMs to provision vehicle access keys to smartphones and wearables through backend APIs. An attacker who compromises the key provisioning backend — through API vulnerability, credential theft, or insider access — can create and distribute valid digital keys for any vehicle in the fleet. Unlike physical key attacks that target one vehicle, backend compromise scales to the entire fleet. The provisioned keys are cryptographically valid and indistinguishable from legitimately issued keys at the vehicle side.
prerequisites: Access to OEM digital key provisioning API
API authentication bypass or credential compromise
detection_methods: Key provisioning event logging with owner notification
Vehicle-side new key acceptance requiring owner presence

patterns.name: UWB Ranging Distance Manipulation
patterns.attack_vector: Manipulating UWB distance bounding measurements to make vehicle believe authorized key is closer than actual
patterns.impact: UWB distance verification defeated, enabling relay-style attack on systems designed to resist relay attacks
known_mitigations: Implement IEEE 802.15.4z HRP-ERDEV with scrambled timestamp sequence
Use multiple UWB anchors for position triangulation
Combine UWB ranging with IMU-based movement detection
patterns.description: UWB (Ultra-Wideband) distance bounding is deployed as a countermeasure against relay attacks, using time-of-flight measurements to verify the key is physically near the vehicle. An attacker attempts to manipulate the UWB ranging by performing early-detect/late-commit attacks on the UWB preamble, injecting signals that cause the receiver to compute a shorter distance than actual. IEEE 802.15.4z with scrambled timestamp sequences makes this significantly harder but not theoretically impossible. This is at the frontier of current research and requires specialized RF equipment and signal processing expertise.
prerequisites: UWB transceiver with sub-nanosecond timing control
Knowledge of target UWB ranging protocol
Specialized signal processing for early-detect attacks

patterns.name: Vehicle Biometric Authentication Spoofing
patterns.attack_vector: Spoofing fingerprint, face, or voice biometric sensors used for vehicle access or personalization
patterns.impact: Biometric access control bypassed, enabling unauthorized vehicle access or driver profile impersonation
known_mitigations: Use multimodal biometric verification requiring two factors
Implement liveness detection to reject spoofed biometric samples
Treat biometrics as convenience feature, not sole security factor
patterns.description: Vehicles increasingly use biometric authentication — fingerprint sensors on door handles, facial recognition cameras, and voice recognition for driver identification. An attacker creates spoofing artifacts: silicone fingerprint molds from lifted prints, 3D-printed face masks or high-resolution photo displays for facial recognition, and recorded/synthesized voice samples. Consumer-grade biometric sensors in vehicles may lack robust liveness detection (checking for pulse, depth, lip movement) that security-focused implementations require. Biometrics work best as a convenience factor paired with possession factor (key, phone).
prerequisites: Captured biometric sample of authorized user

patterns.name: Aftermarket Security System CAN Bus Bypass
patterns.attack_vector: Bypassing aftermarket alarm or immobilizer by directly commanding vehicle ECUs via CAN bus, ignoring the add-on system
patterns.impact: Aftermarket security defeated by operating at a lower protocol layer than it monitors
known_mitigations: Aftermarket systems should interrupt physical circuits, not just monitor CAN
Integrate aftermarket security with OEM immobilizer via authenticated interface
CAN bus authentication (SecOC) preventing unauthorized commands regardless of source
patterns.description: Aftermarket alarm and immobilizer systems typically tap into the vehicle CAN bus to monitor lock/unlock events and control engine start relay. An attacker who understands the CAN bus architecture can bypass these systems by directly commanding the body control module and engine ECU at the CAN protocol level, below the monitoring layer of the aftermarket device. The aftermarket system monitors specific CAN IDs for key events but cannot prevent direct ECU commands it does not recognize. Only systems that physically interrupt circuits (starter relay, fuel pump) resist CAN-level bypass.
prerequisites: Understanding of aftermarket system monitoring approach
detection_methods: Aftermarket system monitoring for unexpected CAN commands
Physical circuit interruption independent of CAN
Cellular alert on bypass attempt detection

patterns.name: Dealer Tool Key Programming Abuse
patterns.attack_vector: Using stolen or cloned dealer diagnostic tool to program new keys into vehicle, bypassing owner authorization
patterns.impact: New key programmed for vehicle without owner knowledge, providing persistent authorized access for theft
known_mitigations: Require OEM backend authorization token for key programming
Implement key programming event notification to registered owner
Log all key programming events with technician identity to OEM cloud
patterns.description: Authorized dealer diagnostic tools include key programming functionality for lost key replacement. An attacker obtains access to a dealer tool — through theft, cloning, or compromising the tool authentication — and uses it to program a new key into the target vehicle's immobilizer. The vehicle accepts the new key as legitimate. Some vehicles allow key programming through the OBD-II port with only seed-key authentication, while others require backend authorization. Stolen dealer tools with valid credentials are regularly used in organized vehicle theft operations.
prerequisites: Physical access to vehicle OBD-II port
detection_methods: Backend authorization requirement for key programming
Owner notification on new key registration
